how-trail-works-en: Four steps, one Trail. A source becomes a candidate; the curator decides what becomes a Neuron; the Neuron answers queries long after the chat that produced it is gone.
how-trail-works-da: Fire trin, én Trail. En kilde bliver til en kandidat; kuratoren beslutter hvad der bliver til en Neuron; Neuronen svarer på spørgsmål længe efter chatten der producerede den er glemt.
memex-desk: A schematic reading of Bush's proposed desk: slanting translucent screens, keyboard, levers, and microfilm reels stored in the base.
tree-vs-graph: The library classifies; the mind associates. Bush's central critique of 20th-century information systems.
trail: The Trail is not a search result. It is a piece of thinking, captured and made permanent.
timeline: A lineage of minds who each carried Bush's idea one step closer to what he described.
three-filters-gate: Candidates flow through three sequential filters. What passes all three is written as a Neuron; what fails any one is routed to a human curator.
filters-correspondence: The same three filters under three different vocabularies: what Bandler & Grinder named in 1975, what thalamic and prefrontal circuits implement biologically, and what Trail's policy engine enforces.
confidence-threshold: Every candidate carries a scalar. Above the threshold, a Neuron is written automatically; below, a human looks at it first.
contradiction-check: Two sources making opposite claims don't silently overwrite each other. The background lint surfaces the conflict, and a curator resolves — sometimes for A, sometimes for B, sometimes by encoding the disagreement itself.
biological-trail-rag: Seven things a brain does. Trail does six of them, at compile time. RAG does one — and only as a weak parallel to spreading activation.
ingest-pipeline: The nine-step path every source takes to become a Neuron. The amber branch is auto-approved; the dashed one waits on a human curator.
query-pipeline: Read time is short because write time was long. The amber box is where the whole architecture pays off: you read an already-compiled Neuron instead of rebuilding an answer from chunks.
rag-vs-trail-pipeline: RAG does all the work for every query and then discards it. Trail splits the work into a compile phase (once) and a read phase (fast and repeatable).
cost-over-time: RAG pays the same toll for every query. Trail front-loads the compile cost and then reads cheaply — the break-even is usually in the first hundred queries per Neuron.
knowledge-compounds-curve: RAG gets no better as you feed it more sources. Trail accelerates because each new source updates every Neuron it touches.
vendor-decision: A two-axis matrix. Trail is the answer when knowledge should both accumulate and leave a persistent, queryable artifact — which is most of the interesting cases.
notebooklm-vs-trail: NotebookLM and Trail agree on source-grounding. They diverge on what the system leaves behind — a chat log that disappears, or a Neuron that keeps working.
artifact-persistence: NotebookLM sessions are bottles thrown into the sea. Trail writes one Neuron and links the next source to it, then the next.
scan-wall-curve: A naive sequential scan crosses the 24-hour scheduling window around 8,000 Neurons and the passes start stacking. The sampled pass stays flat — by design.
sampling-split: Each nightly sample is biased toward recent edits (where contradictions actually live) but reserves room for random long-tail picks so old Neurons never stop being checked.
dreaming-cycle: A scheduled pass that sleeps for most of the day and does one bounded hour of integration work. The analogy to slow-wave consolidation is not metaphorical — it is the same pattern.
scale-tiers: Four representative corpus sizes on a log axis. Each tier stacks its workload state — ingest, lint, queue — above the corpus circle, with amber marks where a workload's behaviour has changed from the previous tier. Infrastructure sits at the top as a growing schematic Machine.
decision-matrix: The same tiers as a decision matrix. Amber cells mark the tier where each workload's strategy changes; charcoal cells mean the previous tier's setting still applies. Ingest is the only column that never changes until the very last tier.
architecture-compile-time-en: Five columns, one architecture. Raw sources enter the ingest funnel; an LLM compiles them into linked Neurons; a human curator gates every update; the persistent knowledge graph grows; fast querying reads already-compiled memory instead of rebuilding from chunks.
architecture-compile-time-da: Fem kolonner, én arkitektur. Råkilder indlæses i funnelen; en LLM kompilerer dem til sammenkædede Neuroner; en menneskelig kurator godkender hver opdatering; den persistente vidensgraf vokser; hurtige forespørgsler læser allerede-kompileret hukommelse i stedet for at genbygge fra chunks.
compile-not-search-en: RAG searches and forgets. Trail compiles and remembers. Two-panel comparison with performance table: RAG pays retrieval cost on every query and discards the result; Trail pays compile cost once at ingest, then reads Neurons cheaply and cumulatively forever.
compile-not-search-da: RAG søger og glemmer. Trail kompilerer og husker. To-panel sammenligning med ydeevnetabel: RAG betaler hentningsomkostning ved hver forespørgsel og kasserer resultatet; Trail betaler kompileringsomkostning én gang ved indlæsning og læser derefter Neuroner billigt og akkumulerende for evigt.
luhmann-bookkeeping-vs-thinking: The 80/20 split that defined Luhmann's working life. Most of his time went into mechanical maintenance of the slip-box — the column an LLM can now take over. The smaller column, the part that produces actual ideas, stays with the human.
zettelkasten-to-trail: Three layers, on each side. Luhmann's slip-boxes had reading notes, permanent cards, and index cards with rules. Trail has sources, Neurons, and a machine-readable schema. The architecture survived seventy years; only the labour shifted.
